Policy Name/Title: Scaling Artificial Intelligence in Health: AI in Health Policy Checklist
Issuing Body: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
Summary: Health-specific AI policy checklist developed by the OECD with the Global Digital Health Partnership, Coalition for Health AI, and OECD AI in Health Expert Group. The framework identifies policy actions for responsibly scaling AI in health across four pillars: establishing enablers, building capacity and capability, implementing guardrails, engaging meaningfully with stakeholders, and deploying trustworthy AI. It is non-prescriptive but intended to help policymakers, technologists, and health workforces identify blind spots and readiness gaps.
Healthcare Implications: Provides an international reference framework for governments and health systems developing AI-in-health strategies, action plans, oversight bodies, procurement expectations, data foundations, workforce capacity, public/provider engagement, and trustworthy deployment practices. It can help benchmark national health-AI readiness and guide policy design around health data access, validation, governance, monitoring, transparency, accountability, equity, and cross-border compatibility.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Policy Name/Title: Guidance on Using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Patient Care
Summary: Professional guidance for doctors using AI in patient care. The guidance covers AI embedded in medical devices, AI scribes, AI tools that manage clinical inboxes, and AI systems that influence clinical decisions. It states that doctors remain responsible for clinical decisions and actions, must understand AI capabilities and limitations, check AI outputs where reasonably practicable, document AI use when it influences clinical decision-making, avoid using AI to impersonate themselves, inform patients when AI is used in care, obtain informed consent in specified higher-risk uses, and ensure privacy, data security, safety, and fairness.
Healthcare Implications: Creates a practical professional-responsibility framework for AI use by New Zealand doctors. It directly affects clinical deployment of AI scribes, decision-support tools, inbox-management systems, and AI-enabled devices by emphasizing clinician accountability, patient transparency, consent, documentation, privacy safeguards, data security, suitability for the patient population, and avoidance of bias or worsening inequities. It is guidance rather than legislation, but it gives clear expectations for safe and ethical AI use in patient-facing medical practice.

Policy Name/Title: CFM Resolution No. 2.454/2026 – Use of Artificial Intelligence in Medicine
Issuing Body: Federal Council of Medicine (Conselho Federal de Medicina)
Summary: Regulates the use of artificial intelligence in medical practice in Brazil. The resolution treats AI as a support tool for medical practice, clinical decision-making, health management, research, and education; requires physicians to remain finally responsible for clinical, diagnostic, therapeutic, and prognostic decisions; requires patient disclosure when AI is used as relevant support in care, diagnosis, or treatment; requires preliminary risk classification, governance processes, human supervision, data protection, monitoring, and bias-mitigation measures for medical AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Creates one of the clearest physician-facing AI governance frameworks in the international module. Brazilian physicians and medical institutions using or developing AI must preserve physician autonomy and final medical judgment, explain AI use to patients, document AI use in medical records when used for decision support, avoid delegating diagnosis or therapeutic communication to AI without human mediation, classify AI systems by risk, and implement internal governance and oversight for safety, quality, ethics, privacy, security, and bias mitigation.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Title: Framework Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust
Issuing Body: South Korean Ministry of Government Legislation
Summary: South Korea’s comprehensive national AI law, enacted in 2025 and enforced beginning January 22, 2026. The Act defines high-impact AI to include AI used in systems for providing and using healthcare services and AI used in the development and use of medical devices or digital medical devices. It establishes national AI governance structures, transparency duties for high-impact and generative AI, safety and risk-management expectations, confirmation procedures for high-impact AI classification, user protection measures, human management and supervision, documentation duties, and impact-assessment encouragement.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare and medical-device AI are expressly included within the Act’s high-impact AI framework, making the law relevant to Korean health systems, medical AI developers, and digital medical device companies. Operators of healthcare AI may need to assess whether systems qualify as high-impact AI, notify users that products or services are AI-based, maintain risk-management and user-protection measures, support explainability where technically feasible, retain safety/reliability documentation, and preserve human management and supervision. Certified digital medical products may also interact with the Act’s deemed-compliance/safe-harbor structure.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public
